author: Stelios Fragkakis
message:
Netdata fixes part 61 (#23081)

* fix(P0, rrdset): reject invalid update intervals

Current bug: BEGIN_V2 can parse update_every=0 and pass it to rrdset_set_update_every_s(), which previously stored it in st->update_every. Later collection code uses st->update_every in modulo/division and can crash with SIGFPE.

Potential bug: rrdset_set_update_every_s() accepts time_t but stores into int32_t, so values above INT32_MAX could truncate into an invalid collection interval.

Functional impact: valid interval changes keep the existing behavior; invalid non-positive or out-of-range changes now leave chart and storage state unchanged. BEGIN_V2 keeps processing the command but uses the retained chart interval for parser state and stream forwarding.

Performance impact: one unlikely integer-range branch is added to the setter and one equality check is added to BEGIN_V2 forwarding. No allocation, loop, lock, or hot sample-path work is added.

Caller impact: pluginsd_begin_v2, pluginsd_replay_begin, pluginsd_replay_end, rrdset_conflict_callback, and dbengine tests keep their valid-value contracts. Invalid caller input is rejected before storage frequency updates. The BEGIN_V2 forwarding path no longer copies rejected interval tokens downstream.

Solution: validate update_every centrally in rrdset_set_update_every_s(), re-read the effective chart interval after BEGIN_V2 setter attempts, and add a mockup unittest covering zero, negative, out-of-range, and valid intervals.
(cherry picked from commit d401179304682d98deab277175beeaaf18e2883a)

* fix(P1, rrdhost): snapshot system_info readers

Finding: MEM-db-rrdhost-003
Priority: P1
Classification: current reachable timing-dependent concurrency/lifetime bug.

Root issue:
rrdhost_update() swaps the contents of host->system_info with a caller-owned
system_info object while holding host->rrdhost_update_lock. The swap is an
aggregate struct swap, so it moves string pointers between the stable host
struct and the temporary receiver struct. The streaming receiver frees its
temporary system_info after rrdhost_find_or_create() returns. Unlocked metadata
readers could therefore observe mixed system_info fields or dereference strings
that were moved into the temporary object and freed.

Solution:
Add rrdhost_system_info_dup() to deep-copy the system_info struct and all string
fields. Metadata readers now take host->rrdhost_update_lock, duplicate the
current system_info, release the lock, use the private snapshot, and free it.
Scalar and fixed-buffer readers copy the needed data while holding the same
lock. Writer ownership and swap behavior are unchanged.

The change also fixes rrdhost_system_info_create() allocation accounting from a
subtract to an add, matching the existing subtract in rrdhost_system_info_free().
The new snapshot create/free pairs require balanced accounting.

Functional impact:
Public API, cloud, streaming, SQLite metadata, label, build-info, and status
outputs keep the same fields and values, but now read a consistent snapshot. No
schema, protocol, endpoint, command-line option, or configuration contract is
changed.

Performance impact:
Adds bounded allocation and string-copy work only on metadata/API/cloud/status
read paths. The update lock is held only for snapshot creation or scalar/fixed
buffer copies; JSON, SQLite, streaming URL encoding, and protobuf serialization
happen after unlock. No metric collection hot loop is changed.

Caller impact:
Snapshot readers covered: stream_connect(), /api/v1/info, streaming Function
output, contexts v2 host JSON, localhost auto-label loading, SQLite host
system_info storage, ACLK node-info build, and build-info population.
rrdhost_ingestion_hops() keeps the same return semantics for localhost,
virtual/no-info, and stored-hop cases while reading under the update lock.
get_daemon_status_fields_from_system_info() and localhost_is_docker() keep their
existing return semantics while copying/comparing under the update lock.

Validation:
- git diff --check passed for all touched files.
- Reference searches for host->system_info, localhost->system_info,
  rrdhost_system_info_to_*, rrdhost_system_info_foreach(), and
  rrdhost_system_info_hops() classified all surviving references.
- git submodule update --init --recursive passed.
- Default CMake configure failed only because the local xenstat pkg-config
  dependency is unavailable.
- CMake configure passed with -DENABLE_PLUGIN_XENSTAT=OFF.
- cmake --build build-MEM-db-rrdhost-003 --target netdata -- -j2 passed.

Reviewer gate:
All six required reviewers approved the full scope. One reviewer output was
incomplete on the first run, so the same full-scope prompt was rerun for that
reviewer and produced a complete approval.

(cherry picked from commit 0e14c6968ac773a17eff99b05c5c46d5af92852f)

* fix(P1, exporting): synchronize simple readiness flag

Issue: simple_connector_worker() read the plain int instance->data_is_ready before taking instance->mutex, while producer formatting and shutdown signaling write the same field under that mutex.

Classification: current data race / undefined behavior for configured simple exporting connectors running concurrently with producer or shutdown paths.

Solution: move the existing netdata_mutex_lock(&instance->mutex) before the first data_is_ready sample so all worker reads and clears use the same synchronization contract as the writers.

Functional impact: exported payloads, headers, retry behavior, response handling, data-loss accounting, buffer ownership, TLS setup, and connector dispatch are unchanged; the readiness sample is now race-free.

Performance impact: no new lock acquisition and no network, TLS, connect, send, receive, or response handling work moves under the mutex; the same lock is acquired a few instructions earlier.

Caller impact: graphite, JSON, OpenTSDB, and Prometheus remote write still assign simple_connector_worker(), and init_connectors still launches instance->worker with the same instance pointer.

Validation: git diff --check; same-pattern rg scans; low-priority submodule update; low-priority CMake configure with ENABLE_PLUGIN_XENSTAT=OFF; low-priority send_data.o build; low-priority netdata target build.

Reviewer gate: six read-only reviewers semantically approved the exporting fix; a reported side-scope blocker was verified false because the actual diff against HEAD contains only src/exporting/send_data.c.

(cherry picked from commit daf368673eaf53273f89ee4ceb7d6c72ebbbd7cd)

* fix(P1, health): stabilize alert chart lifetime

Current vs potential: potential memory-safety race. Source review confirms concurrent alert unlink paths can clear rc->rrdset under the chart alerts spinlock while the health event loop and alert variable lookup paths continue using an earlier unprotected chart pointer.

Solution: acquire the linked chart by alert chart id, validate rc->rrdset == st under st->alerts.spinlock, and pass the stable RRDSET pointer through event-loop checks, DB lookup, expression evaluation, and variable lookup. Keep alert-log insertion, notification dispatch, DB queries, and expression evaluation outside the chart alerts spinlock.

Functional impact: normal linked-alert behavior is unchanged. Concurrently detached alerts are skipped or handled through the existing expression-error path instead of dereferencing a stale or cleared chart link. The public rrdcalc_rrdset_read_lock() contract is preserved.

Performance impact: adds bounded chart dictionary acquisition and short read-lock validation around alert evaluation points. The lock is not held across expensive work, so contention is limited to link validation and alert-entry field copying.

Caller impact: updates only health event-loop helpers and direct health variable lookup users. Existing rrdcalc_rrdset_read_lock() callers keep the same return/unlock contract. The inline helper includes the required rrdset acquisition prototypes so translation units that see rrdcalc.h before rrdset-index-id.h compile cleanly.

Validation: git diff --check passed; low-priority cmake --build build --target netdata -j2 passed. The first controller build caught the missing declaration-order dependency in rrdcalc.h; the final commit includes that fix and the rerun passed.

Reviewer gate: all six required read-only reviews completed successfully and approved the fix as production-grade and necessary.

(cherry picked from commit 9198f19749f5181993d9e853b5ed6656fd018adb)

* P1 health: retain chart handles for lookup results

Issue: alert variable lookup stored raw RRDSET pointers in scored lookup results, then released the acquired chart handle before the selected result was logged or serialized. A chart removed between result collection and result reporting could leave source_st pointing at freed chart memory.

Solution: each scored result now stores its own acquired chart handle. The selected result remains valid through logging and JSON emission, then all result handles are released on the common exit path.

Bug status: current bug. The code exercised this path today when variable lookup collects candidates from contexts, host variables, or running alerts before choosing the best scored result.

Functional impact: no intended change to lookup selection or output. If a chart disappears during lookup, that candidate is skipped instead of storing a stale pointer.

Performance impact: small and localized. Candidate results now acquire/release chart handles; alert variable lookup is not a high-frequency ingestion path, and this cost buys a valid lifetime contract.

(cherry picked from commit be2120e81d774851a8799d013ba2a9b1abb206ed)

* P3 unittest: guard time_t range test portability

Issue: the update_every unit test constructed INT32_MAX + 1 as a time_t value unconditionally. On platforms where time_t is 32-bit, that test expression cannot represent the intended out-of-range value portably.

Solution: run the out-of-range branch only when time_t is wider than int32_t, and build the test value with an increment from INT32_MAX in that wider type.

Bug status: current portability bug in the test code on 32-bit time_t platforms.

Functional impact: no product behavior change. The runtime validation remains covered where the platform can represent the invalid value.

Performance impact: none; unit-test-only change.

(cherry picked from commit 71c6171e257a3e6aaadb8034648f28e4c89672ab)

* P2 rrdset: ignore empty dimensions in first retention

Finding: MEM-db-rrdset-005 (P2)

Root cause:
rrddim_first_entry_s() returns 0 when a dimension has no retained data.
rrdset_first_entry_s() accepted that absence sentinel into a LONG_MAX-seeded
minimum, so one empty dimension masked every retained sibling timestamp.

Classification and reachability:
This is a current, state-dependent bug. DBENGINE creates a per-dimension metric
with zero retention, and dimension publication is separate from first sample
storage. A mature chart can therefore contain retained dimensions plus a newly
empty dimension without a race or invalid state.

Solution:
Skip zero dimension results while reducing the chart first-entry timestamp,
using the predicate already present in rrdset_first_entry_s_of_tier(). No-data
and all-empty charts still return 0, while all-retained charts keep the same
minimum.

Functional and caller impact:
- rrdset2json() reports the real mixed-state first_entry and duration.
- rrdcalc_isrunnable() applies the real retained lower bound instead of a zero
  bypass.
- run_test() is unchanged because existing tests populate every dimension.
- Signatures, ABI, JSON schema, storage, streaming, ownership, locks,
  concurrency, and error paths are unchanged.

Performance:
The existing O(dimensions) traversal gains one scalar nonzero test per
dimension, with no allocation, I/O, syscall, or additional lock.

Validation:
- focused rrdset.c object and full netdata builds passed at low priority
- netdata -W unittest passed with ALL TESTS PASSED
- compiled real-function mixed-input probe produced the corrected minimum
- diff, reference, same-pattern, and sensitive-data checks passed
- all six required source reviewers approved the final change

(cherry picked from commit 75627d37645311ef7927a9ea627d1bec7096d4e2)

---------

Co-authored-by: Costa Tsaousis <costa@netdata.cloud>

author: Stelios Fragkakis
message:
Netdata fixes part 57 (#23077)

* fix(weights): propagate parallel query stop flags

Parallel weights queries copy the request state into a worker-local query_weights_data before walking host metrics. The metric callback sets timed_out or interrupted on that local copy, while web_api_v12_weights() and sibling workers observe the original request state. That is a current bug when a parallel query times out or is interrupted inside metric processing: the stop condition can be lost, traversal can continue, and partial results can be reported as successful.

Route stop-state reads and writes through the shared parent query_weights_data when a worker-local copy is active. Also make the context callback convert a shared stop flag back to -1 so the host/context traversal contract still stops after a metric callback requested cancellation.

Functional impact: no intended change for successful queries. Timeout and interrupt handling now matches the existing API contract in the parallel path instead of being swallowed by worker-local copies.

Performance impact: steady-state cost is limited to relaxed atomic stop checks and helper indirection in the metric callback. Under cancellation, work can stop earlier because sibling workers observe the shared flag.

(cherry picked from commit 737ddbf8b690f6acf3c7d7dd6dfa8730c7bad803)

* fix(P1): guard weights host array zero growth

Finding ID: MEM-web-api-queries-007

Priority: P1

Classification: current source defect with potential runtime trigger. The invalid zero-capacity invariant exists in the current DBENGINE parallel weights path; runtime exposure needs a zero host-count snapshot followed by a queryable host reaching the later enumeration.

Root issue: query_scope_foreach_host_parallel initialized hosts_array_capacity from dictionary_entries(rrdhost_root_index). When that snapshot was zero, weights_count_node_callback doubled zero capacity to zero and then wrote hosts_array[0], risking zero-size realloc behavior and an out-of-bounds write.

Solution: make the initial hosts_array capacity at least one and make the growth branch convert zero capacity to one before reallocating. Non-zero capacities still use the existing doubling behavior.

Functional impact: no public API, filtering, traversal, scheduling, output, locking, or worker-dispatch behavior changes. Only the broken zero-capacity storage edge case is guarded.

Performance impact: normal non-zero host snapshots keep the same allocation size and doubling behavior. The only added work is a cold-branch conditional on growth and one pointer slot in the zero-snapshot edge case.

Caller impact: web_api_v12_weights remains the convergence point for v1/v2/v3 HTTP weights and MCP weights requests. The single-host v1 path and multidimensional shortcut remain bypassed as before. DBENGINE parallel worker dispatch receives the same host slices, now backed by valid storage.

Validation: git diff --check passed. CMake configured with ENABLE_PLUGIN_XENSTAT=OFF because xenstat is unavailable on this worker. The netdata target built successfully; weights.c, v1/v2 API weights callers, MCP weights caller, and the netdata link were covered.

Reviewer gate: all six required read-only reviews approved the patch as production grade and necessary; no blocker was raised.

(cherry picked from commit e5008bcdd6732ca6c7abcb526bba8a618f780ee5)

* fix(P1, rrdfunctions): serialize duplicate transactions

MEM-db-rrdfunctions-002

Issue/current-potential: current for callers that can reuse supplied transaction IDs; potential-only for generated-UUID callers.

Solution: remove the shared non-atomic duplicate flag, classify duplicate keys via the dictionary conflict callback under the dictionary index write lock, and release duplicate local acquisitions through the current host dictionary.

Functional impact: duplicate transactions keep returning HTTP 400 with the existing Duplicate transaction response; non-duplicate sync, async, cancel, progress, shutdown, and result callback paths are unchanged.

Performance impact: no new lookup or lock on the non-duplicate path; duplicate handling adds one stack-local flag write while already in dictionary conflict handling.

Caller impact: API v1 function/config, bearer token, stream execute, MCP function execution, MCP registry info, dyncfg echo, and dyncfg unittest keep the same rrd_function_run contract.

Validation: git diff --check passed; targeted same-pattern scans passed; low-priority CMake configure passed; low-priority focused rrdfunctions-inflight object build passed.

Reviewer gate: six full-scope read-only reviewer outputs semantically approved with no blockers; an incomplete reviewer output was rejected and rerun, and the final rerun produced an explicit mergeable/no-blockers verdict.

(cherry picked from commit b620b3a4f971639c057fe68cc17fe98a29d03001)

* P1 weights: guard host array allocation overflow

Issue: query weight host collection multiplied host counts by sizeof(RRDHOST *) manually for allocation and growth. Extremely large or corrupted host counts could wrap the allocation size and leave a smaller buffer than the subsequent host pointer writes expect.

Solution: use reallocarray() for both initial allocation and growth, and add an explicit capacity-doubling overflow guard before growing the array.

Bug status: potential bug. Normal host counts are far below the limit, but allocation code must remain correct under extreme or corrupted counts.

Functional impact: no intended behavior change for valid host counts. Overflow now aborts cleanly through fatal() instead of risking memory corruption.

Performance impact: none in practice. reallocarray() performs checked multiplication during rare allocation/growth events, not per sample.

(cherry picked from commit ab169f8fc2ca586e60e5653d541a8b8c50e201b7)

---------

Co-authored-by: Costa Tsaousis <costa@netdata.cloud>

author: Stelios Fragkakis
message:
Netdata fixes part 59 (#23080)

* fix(P0, db): bound rrdset interpolation gaps

Current/potential bug: DBENGINE skipped the stale stored-gap reset, so a chart whose stored timestamp lagged far behind the collection timestamp could enter interpolation with an enormous timestamp catch-up loop.

Functional impact: normal cadence behavior and non-DBENGINE thresholds are unchanged; DBENGINE now resets collection state after the existing 60-interval stale gap floor instead of replaying synthetic points across an excessive gap.

Performance impact: prevents CPU livelock on large DBENGINE gaps; normal operation adds only constant-time arithmetic and one loop-budget decrement in the interpolation path.

Caller impact: rrdset_timed_done callers keep the same signature and storage contract; abnormal stale DBENGINE gaps use the existing collection reset flow.

Solution: reuse the existing max update gap threshold, apply the 60-interval floor to DBENGINE stored-gap reset, and add an exact precomputed loop iteration budget.
(cherry picked from commit 30a5b7ec57ff5ccf713766e3f968a836d944df39)

* fix(P1): make RAM metric reads race-free

Issue: MEM-db-ram-002 is a current exercised C data race. RAM collection writes sample slots and mem_metric_handle control fields while web/export/ML/backfill/streaming/context readers can read them without st->data_collection_lock.

Potential/coupled scope: mh->data pointer access is fixed in the same helper boundary because slot helpers must load it before addressing data[slot]. Unit-test-only rd->db.data[] reads are excluded from the runtime race scope.

Solution: route private RAM backend accesses to data, data slots, counter, entries, current_entry, last_updated_s, and update_every_s through relaxed atomic helpers in src/database/ram/rrddim_mem.c. Keep mh->rd acquire/release helpers unchanged.

Functional impact: no intended user-visible change. Ring slots, packed storage_number values, query point shape, timestamp progression, handle ownership, and best-effort no-snapshot semantics are preserved.

Performance impact: relaxed scalar, pointer, and sample loads/stores only. No locks, blocking, syscalls, allocations, public API changes, config changes, or storage format changes are introduced.

Caller impact: storage_engine RAM store/flush/update-frequency and query/retention dispatch paths keep the same signatures and return shapes; caller surfaces include web queries, exporting, ML, backfill, streaming, context retention, and rrddim retention helpers.

Validation: git diff --check passed; focused low-priority compile of src/database/ram/rrddim_mem.c passed; same-pattern scans leave only helper implementations and unit-test-only rd->db.data[] reads outside the private RAM backend file.

Reviewer gate: six required read-only reviewer outputs semantically approved the full-scope diff with no source-backed blockers.

(cherry picked from commit 1721e0edfe790d8bd5452ac220b9bf8817a3caec)

* P2 stop dbengine datafile force deletion

dbengine datafile deletion uses df->users.lockers as the lifetime guard for readers, retention, indexing, and page-cache paths that still hold struct rrdengine_datafile pointers. The old force-delete timeout could make datafile_acquire_for_deletion() return true while lockers were still present after open-cache pages had drained.

datafile_delete() treats that true result as permission to remove the datafile from the Judy list, destroy the journal/data files, zero the structures, and free both allocations. Any remaining locker could then dereference zeroed or freed datafile state.

Current vs potential: this is a current runtime rotation path. The unsafe trigger requires lockers to remain after the timeout, so the datafile deletion path is current and the stuck-locker timing condition is potential.

Remove the force-delete timeout state and branch, and simplify the internal acquire-for-deletion API so deletion is authorized only when writers are stopped and lockers are zero. The existing datafile_delete() retry-and-return path remains the backoff mechanism when lockers have not drained.

Functional impact: valid deletion is unchanged when lockers drain. If lockers remain, retention cleanup is deferred to a later rotation instead of freeing live datafile structures.

Performance impact: no hot-path cost; the unhappy deletion path does less timeout bookkeeping and keeps the existing one-second sleep with a 30-attempt cap.

(cherry picked from commit 6406d97afeb113f1fde0c594504b84e4e6a544ff)

* fix(dbengine): bound gorilla disk buffer chains

Gorilla pages loaded from disk are copied into one contiguous raw.data buffer. Their serialized header.next fields are not valid process pointers after load; only their null or non-null state indicates whether another fixed-size gorilla buffer follows. gorilla_buffer_patch() trusted that state without knowing the copied page's buffer count, so a malformed page whose last serialized buffer had a non-null next field could advance one buffer past raw.data, write a patched next pointer out of bounds, and then read entries from out-of-bounds memory.

This is a current bug on malformed or corrupt DBEngine gorilla pages loaded from disk or replicated page bytes. Valid serialized pages terminate within their copied buffer count and are not intended to change.

Pass the known fixed-size buffer count into gorilla_buffer_patch(), return false before any advance beyond that count, and have pgd_create_from_disk_data() free the temporary disk-created page and return PGD_EMPTY on malformed chains. Emit an error log on that malformed-chain rejection path so corruption is not silent. Add a regression test that corrupts the last serialized gorilla buffer's next field and verifies that the page is rejected.

Spell serialized gorilla buffer stepping as byte arithmetic through unsigned char and void pointers. This keeps the exact RRDENG_GORILLA_32BIT_BUFFER_SIZE stride while avoiding reinterpret_cast-based type punning in the new disk-chain code and test setup.

Functional impact: valid gorilla page decoding, entry counting, pointer patching, memory ownership, and collector-created page behavior are unchanged. Malformed chains now become empty/invalid pages instead of causing out-of-bounds reads and writes, and emit one error log before returning PGD_EMPTY.

Performance impact: disk page loading pays one counter comparison per serialized gorilla buffer. The byte-stride spelling compiles to equivalent address arithmetic. The new log executes only on malformed-chain rejection; metric collection, writer, and reader hot paths are unchanged.

(cherry picked from commit 725669acfd99dd92905e38166c7c3c54c73b0af2)

* fix(P1, dbengine): make page end time access atomic

Issue: pgc_page_fix_end_time_s() repaired published PGC page end_time_s with a plain store while concurrent readers and the hot-page extension path accessed the same field.

Current vs potential: current conditional source bug. The pagecache repair path can run during query lookup on an acquired page, while collection, migration, retention, and query paths can read the same page metadata. The previous mixed plain and atomic accesses were undefined behavior when those paths overlapped.

Solution: use relaxed atomic loads and stores for post-publication page->end_time_s accesses in cache.c. Leave pre-publication initialization plain and return the requested fixed end time from pgc_page_fix_end_time_s().

Functional impact: no intended behavior change. Existing page selection, repair, retention, eviction, migration, query, disk-format, and last-writer-wins semantics are preserved.

Performance impact: negligible. The change adds relaxed scalar atomic operations only, with no locks, allocations, syscalls, I/O, loops, or retries.

Security impact: positive concurrency and memory-safety impact by removing undefined behavior from shared page metadata. No input, privilege, protocol, or configuration surface changes.

Caller impact: page_find_and_acquire_once(), pgc_page_end_time_s(), pgc_page_fix_end_time_s(), and pgc_open_cache_to_journal_v2() keep their return, side-effect, lock, lifetime, ownership, and data-format contracts. Callers observe the same scalar values through race-free access.

Validation: git diff --check passed for src/database/engine/cache.c; submodules initialized; CMake configure passed with local Xen plugin disabled; cmake --build build --target src/database/engine/cache.o -j 2 passed.

Reviewer gate: six required reviewers completed successfully and gave clear final approvals.

(cherry picked from commit 04edf672b6c53143004730ea4ece8aaecb9c0fcf)

* fix(P1, dbengine): leave MRG allocated with referenced metrics

Issue: mrg_destroy() counted metrics that failed deletion acquisition but still freed their UUIDs, METRIC allocations, Judy arrays, partition ARALs, and MRG state. Any holder that kept one of those metrics after teardown would point into freed storage.

Folded findings: MEM-dbengine-mrg-002 and MEM-dbengine-core-002 are the same unique issue: same mrg_destroy() root cause, same reported locations, and same lifetime contract failure.

Current vs potential: source-current, trigger-potential. The unsafe source path exists today; a deterministic normal-production trigger was not proven because the daemon cleanup caller is ASAN-only, but tests and ASAN shutdown can exercise mrg_destroy().

Solution: claim all unreferenced metrics with refcount deletion acquisition before freeing. If any metric remains referenced, restore the claimed metrics to refcount zero, free only the temporary claim vector, unlock partitions, return the referenced count, and leave the full MRG allocation graph alive. If no metrics are referenced, free metrics, Judy arrays, ARALs, stats, and MRG as before.

Functional impact: normal runtime behavior and no-reference destroy behavior are unchanged. Referenced teardown now preserves MRG-owned state instead of freeing underneath active holders.

Performance impact: no hot-path cost. The added all-partition locking and temporary claim-vector allocation run only during MRG teardown.

Caller impact: mrg_destroy() keeps returning the referenced metric count. The changed non-zero return contract is now explicit: when non-zero, MRG remains allocated and can be retried after references are released.

Validation: git diff --check passed; focused same-pattern scan of mrg_destroy() passed; low-priority CMake configure passed; low-priority netdata build with -j2 passed; low-priority netdata -W mrgtest passed and ended with DBENGINE METRIC: all tests passed.

Reviewer gate: required reviewer process gate passed and all six required reviewer outputs ended with final approval verdicts. The first minimax output was incomplete and was rerun with the full scope before accepting the gate.

Side finding: uuidmap_destroy() appears to have a similar referenced-entry destroy pattern and was recorded separately as DISC-uuidmap-destroy-referenced-free-20260707; it is not fixed here to keep this commit surgical.

(cherry picked from commit 65aa3289b5817d59bdc9de166ca7d13d01014b9c)

* P2 dbengine: guard LZ4 size conversions

Finding MEM-dbengine-compression-003 exposed a latent contract mismatch: dbengine_decompress() accepts size_t lengths but narrows them to the int parameters required by LZ4. The sole current caller validates extents to a 502272-byte nominal uncompressed cap, so valid data cannot trigger it; a future caller or extent-limit change could pass an unrepresentable size.

Reject LZ4 source or destination sizes above INT_MAX before the casts, log the sizes, and return the existing decompression failure sentinel that the caller maps to have_read_error. Keep the always-active compression bound assertion unchanged: its largest accepted input has a 2122219149-byte bound, below INT_MAX.

Also remove the redundant size_t-to-int-to-size_t cast around dbengine_compress() in datafile_extent_build(). Valid compression bytes, fallback, disk format, CRC/accounting, ownership, lifetimes, locking, concurrency, and caller error contracts are unchanged. LZ4 reads gain one unlikely two-comparison branch; writes gain no work.

Validated with focused optimized object builds, strict format-warning compilation, an ASan/UBSan actual-source boundary probe, a full optimized/LTO daemon link, and the isolated full internal suite including DBEngine. All six required reviewers completed and approved.

(cherry picked from commit 47cd3fb16786e3b89c1fb765dfb6b3c4b015f7b6)

* fix(gorilla): remove unbounded unpatched helpers

Gorilla disk pages are patched through gorilla_buffer_patch(), which receives the copied page buffer count and rejects a serialized chain before it can step past that count.

The remaining gorilla_buffer_unpatched_nbuffers() and gorilla_buffer_unpatched_nbytes() helpers had no in-tree callers, but they still exposed the old contract: trust any non-null serialized header.next value and advance by one fixed-size Gorilla buffer with no page-size bound. Current runtime behavior was unaffected because no code called them; the potential impact was a future caller reusing the helpers on malformed copied page data and reading past the page buffer.

Remove the unused helper declarations and definitions so the only surviving untrusted disk-chain walker is the bounded gorilla_buffer_patch() path.

Functional impact: valid Gorilla page loading, writer, reader, serialization, entry counting, and disk patching behavior are unchanged. The only removed surface is unused internal helper API.

Performance impact: no runtime cost; deletion-only.

Security impact: removes an unused internal API that could reintroduce an out-of-bounds read on malformed serialized Gorilla page chains.

Validation: git diff --check; reference search for removed helper names; built gorilla.cc, page.c, and libnetdata.

Review: required independent source review approved the deletion.
(cherry picked from commit 478b922bcc5426f4b5a8dcd41f8863e7c740fd86)

* P1 dbengine: skip datafiles that cannot be acquired at shutdown

finalize_data_files() waited up to one second for each datafile's lockers to drain, then closed and freed the datafile anyway - the same unsafe free the force-deletion removal eliminated from the rotation path. When acquisition keeps failing, log an error and skip the datafile: leaking it at shutdown is safer than freeing it under its remaining users.

* P1 dbengine: keep MRG allocator teardown under partition locks

mrg_destroy() released all partition locks before destroying the partition ARALs, and metric_add_and_acquire() allocated from them before taking the partition lock, so a racing creator could allocate from a destroyed allocator and re-populate freed Judy arrays. Destroy the ARALs while still holding all partition locks, and allocate the METRIC at its point of use under the partition write lock - mirroring the uuidmap teardown pattern. This also drops the allocate-then-free round-trip on the duplicate-metric path.

---------

Co-authored-by: Costa Tsaousis <costa@netdata.cloud>

author: Stelios Fragkakis
message:
Netdata fixes part 58 (#23078)

* fix(threads): keep wrappers alive after failed joins

nd_thread_join() set JOINED before calling uv_thread_join() and then freed the ND_THREAD wrapper even when uv_thread_join() returned an error. If the worker had not yet reached NETDATA_THREAD_STATUS_FINISHED, the worker could still reach nd_thread_exit() through its thread-local wrapper pointer while the joiner had already freed that wrapper. This is a current source-level lifetime bug on failed joins; the concrete trigger depends on the platform and libuv returning a join error before the worker has finished Netdata-side cleanup.

The fix preserves the existing successful join path. On a failed join, nd_thread_join() now waits for the existing UV_EINVAL fast-finish case as before, then frees the wrapper only if the worker is known FINISHED. If the worker is not proven finished, it clears JOINED and returns the original join error, leaving the wrapper alive for the worker or a later cleanup path. The coupled Windows hardware-info cleanup comment is updated so it no longer claims failed joins free the wrapper.

Functional impact: success paths and FINISHED-after-UV_EINVAL cleanup remain unchanged. The failed-not-finished path changes from unsafe free to a retained wrapper and the same nonzero return code. This may retain memory on an exceptional failed join, which is intentionally safer than use-after-free.

Performance impact: none on normal thread execution and negligible on failed joins. The new work is one FINISHED status check and one JOINED clear only after uv_thread_join() has already failed.

Caller impact: callers that ignore or log the nonzero return still observe a nonzero failure. Callers that clean local resources after a failed join are no longer paired with a freed thread wrapper that a live worker may still use. The public nd_thread_join() signature and return convention are unchanged.

(cherry picked from commit b6dfb8ba73e030d06d08833227180adba1182171)

* fix(P0, spawn-server): harden nofork request framing

Current bug: the nofork request receiver used single recvmsg() calls on a SOCK_STREAM socket and trusted any positive return as a complete header or payload. A short first read could leave request_id, env_size, argv_size, data_size, or type uninitialized before use. A short second read could leave encoded argv/envp buffers partially filled, and argv_decode() used unbounded strlen() on those buffers.

Potential bug: malformed or partial stream frames could drive bad allocation sizes, dispatch decisions, descriptor leaks on rejection, or strlen() over-read past the received buffer. Truncated ancillary data also needed bounded descriptor cleanup before request ownership existed.

Functional impact: valid private spawn-server requests and pings keep the same behavior. Invalid, incomplete, truncated-control, or malformed encoded-vector frames are rejected earlier, with received file descriptors closed on every reject path.

Performance impact: normal valid traffic remains one request send and two request receives. Short-transfer loops only process remaining bytes, argv/envp decoding scans the same buffers with bounded memchr(), and the transfer helpers add no heap allocations.

Caller impact: spawn_server_event_loop(), spawn_server_exec(), and spawn_server_create_listening_socket() keep their existing contracts. Private helper callers now receive true only after complete send/receive operations, and cleanup ownership remains local until SPAWN_REQUEST handoff.

Solution: add bounded full send/receive helpers, strict argv/envp decode validation, SCM_RIGHTS cleanup for failed receives and crafted pings, descriptor ownership cleanup before auth/options rejection, and bounded ancillary-data walking for truncated control messages.
(cherry picked from commit 2628a8dffae37c6d46d4a2985ac03da29deeeafe)

* fix(P1, claim): honor explicit insecure env option

Current bug: automatic environment claiming inverted the NETDATA_EXTRA_CLAIM_OPTS -insecure check. The previous strstr(..., "-insecure") == 0 condition was true when the option was absent, so any non-empty extra-claim option string without -insecure disabled TLS peer and host verification during the claim request.

Functional impact: unset and empty NETDATA_EXTRA_CLAIM_OPTS remain unchanged. Values containing -insecure now enable insecure claiming as documented. Non-empty values without -insecure now keep TLS verification enabled and avoid persisting an accidental insecure=yes value into cloud.conf, which also prevents that wrong setting from affecting later Cloud connection certificate handling.

Performance impact: unchanged. The code still performs one strstr scan and one pointer comparison on the environment option string.

Caller impact: claim_agent_from_environment keeps the same return contract and still returns claim_agent success or failure. claim_agent_automatically and load_claiming_state keep their automatic-claim flow; only the security mode passed to claim_agent changes for the two inverted input cases.

Solution: compare the strstr result against NULL for presence, preserving the existing substring-based option recognition while correcting the inverted absence check.
(cherry picked from commit 10b487a14110f3fda1b4ca7239fe8416e153a924)

* fix(P1, log): synchronize pending flood message drain

Issue: nd_logger() read nd_log.sources[source].pending_msg before taking source->limits.spinlock, while nd_log_limit_reached() frees and replaces the same pointer under that spinlock.

Current vs potential: current. Daemon and collector logging route through nd_logger() with flood protection enabled, so concurrent log calls can exercise the unlocked read and locked writer today.

Solution: remove the unlocked pending_msg precheck. The drain now tries the existing non-blocking source limit spinlock first, reads and clears pending_msg and pending_msgid under the lock, then logs and frees the captured pointer after unlocking.

Functional impact: no intended user-visible change. Deferred flood-protection messages are still opportunistic, emitted at most once, and remain pending for a later log call if the trylock fails.

Performance impact: one additional non-blocking trylock attempt per completed nd_logger() call in the no-pending-message case. No blocking wait is added, and the cost is after priority filtering and log emission setup.

Security impact: removes process-local C data-race undefined behavior on a heap pointer. No new input, file, socket, credential, or privilege surface is added.

Caller impact: nd_logger() is static and still has the same three direct callers: netdata_logger(), netdata_logger_with_limit(), and netdata_logger_fatal(). Their return guarantees and side effects are unchanged; health logging continues through netdata_logger().

Validation: git diff --check -- src/libnetdata/log/nd_log.c passed; same-failure rg confirmed no remaining unlocked pending_msg implementation read; low-priority CMake configure passed; low-priority libnetdata build with -j2 passed and compiled nd_log.c.

Reviewer gate: required reviewer process gate passed, the first semantic read failed closed on incomplete verdicts, the full-scope rerun passed, and all six required reviewer outputs ended with final approval verdicts.

(cherry picked from commit acf22e898fb98dc525e24c968cb84baac35dc67d)

* P2 claim: match insecure option as a token

Issue: claim_agent_from_environment() detected -insecure with strstr() over NETDATA_EXTRA_CLAIM_OPTS. Any option containing that substring could enable insecure claim behavior unintentionally.

Solution: parse the environment value as whitespace-separated tokens and enable insecure mode only when one token exactly equals -insecure.

Bug status: current bug if an operator supplies another extra option containing the substring -insecure.

Functional impact: intended narrowing of accepted input. The documented space-separated -insecure option still works; substring matches no longer do.

Performance impact: negligible. The environment string is tiny and parsed once during claim startup.

(cherry picked from commit 3d5273f65d91d66ce1cb92527c431ba0a96c752f)

* P2 threads: keep failed-join wrappers discoverable

nd_thread_join_threads() unlinks each wrapper from the exited list before joining it, so a failed join that keeps the wrapper alive left it on no list, unreachable to any later cleanup pass. Report the release state from an internal join helper, keep failed wrappers on a local list so the reaping loop still drains, and restore them to the exited list after the pass.

The retained wrapper keeps the JOINED flag (CAS ownership) until it is discoverable or back with its direct owner, so no concurrent joiner can free it while it is parked on the local list.

---------

Co-authored-by: Costa Tsaousis <costa@netdata.cloud>

author: Costa Tsaousis
message: fix false PARTIAL annotations on complete data (#23114)

author: Stelios Fragkakis
message:
Netdata fixes part 56 (#23072)

* P0 fix windows spawn log forwarder fd ownership

Windows spawn stderr forwarding used the numeric stderr fd as both the log-forwarder entry identity and the spawn teardown fallback close target.

This is a current Windows bug. The forwarder worker can close and free the stderr entry after child EOF or HUP, while spawn teardown still keeps the old fd number. If that number has already been recycled by another file, socket, or pipe, the teardown fallback close can close the unrelated descriptor and cause EBADF. The old fd-number lookup could also mark a different live child entry deleted after fd reuse.

Make log-forwarder adoption return a stable token and store it in the Windows spawn instance. After successful adoption, teardown retires the token only and never raw-closes the adopted fd number. If adoption fails, teardown keeps the previous caller-owned close behavior.

Functional impact: valid child stderr forwarding is unchanged; only stale teardown behavior changes from raw close of an unowned fd number to token retirement or no-op if the forwarder already closed it.

Performance impact: negligible; token generation adds one uint64 increment per adopted fd and token lookup keeps the same O(n) list scan as the previous fd lookup.

(cherry picked from commit 349a1033c00410bc6f8f0d020749b414b6b8d31a)

* P1 fix windows perflib provider bounds

Windows perflib data comes from provider-controlled registry blobs, but the parser used PERF_DATA_BLOCK.TotalByteLength from the blob as the global bounds anchor. getPerformanceData discarded the actual byte count returned by RegQueryValueEx, so an over-reported TotalByteLength could make later validators approve reads beyond the returned buffer.

This is a current Windows malformed-provider bug. Existing Windows collectors exercise this parser today; well-formed providers are unaffected, while malformed providers could crash the agent or leak adjacent bytes through instance names and labels.

Thread the actual byte count into getDataBlock, reject too-small returned data, clamp TotalByteLength down to the returned byte count before existing validators use it, and reject impossible data-block lengths. Also validate PERF_INSTANCE_DEFINITION NameOffset and NameLength inside the instance block before getInstanceName converts the provider string.

Functional impact: valid perflib data is parsed the same way; malformed over-reported or out-of-span provider data now fails validation instead of being read.

Performance impact: negligible; the fix adds only integer bounds checks and one byte-count out parameter on the existing registry-query path.

(cherry picked from commit 074ff3b212488151dbab772b53fe79e3882fe9a3)

* P1 fix windows sensor string conversion bound

Windows GetSensors converted sensor-provided VT_LPWSTR strings into char value[8192] with wcstombs(..., wcslen(src)). The third argument is the destination byte capacity, not the source WCHAR count, so a long sensor string could overflow the stack buffer and the failure path returned before clearing the PROPVARIANT.

This is a current Windows plugin bug because GetSensors reads sensor friendly-name, model, and manufacturer properties during sensor discovery. The trigger requires an unusually long or conversion-failing sensor property.

Use sizeof(value) - 1 as the wcstombs destination capacity, test for (size_t)-1, clear the PROPVARIANT on conversion failure, and keep explicit NUL termination after successful conversion.

Functional impact: normal sensor strings are unchanged; oversized strings are truncated to the fixed local buffer instead of corrupting the stack, and conversion failures clean up before returning no value.

Performance impact: none beyond replacing a wcslen scan with the bounded conversion already required for the copy.

(cherry picked from commit 56a54b9dc809a30940da662caf49aa513eeed5d9)

* P2 guard windows perflib parent traversal

The Windows perflib dump path walks parent instances to emit diagnostic JSON labels. The old loop used the original instance's ParentObjectTitleIndex on every iteration while advancing a separate current-instance pointer, so multi-level parent chains could look up the wrong object type.

The same path also trusted getObjectTypeByIndex() and getInstanceByPosition() results before using them for label output. Missing or malformed parent references could therefore crash the dump command through a NULL object dereference or return a wrong restarted instance from getInstanceByPosition().

Current vs potential: this is current in the Windows perflib dump command, not the daemon collection hot path. The unsafe trigger requires malformed or unexpected parent-chain data from a provider, so normal valid providers keep the same behavior.

Fix the parent lookup to use the current parent-chain instance, stop label traversal when the parent object or instance cannot be resolved, and make getInstanceByPosition() return NULL instead of restarting from the first instance after an intermediate lookup failure.

Functional impact: valid one-level parent labels are unchanged, valid multi-level parent labels become correct, and malformed chains now produce partial valid JSON instead of crashing or emitting wrong parent labels.

Performance impact: negligible in a diagnostic-only path; the change adds simple NULL checks and skips one unused counter-block lookup on successful final instance resolution.

(cherry picked from commit fe96f2720471d578bd76d4b02cc04d06c3ea94b2)

* P1 spawn: read log forwarder state under lock

Issue: log_forwarder_del_and_close_token() read lf->running before taking the forwarder spinlock. The worker thread updates that state under the same lock, so the unlocked read could race with shutdown and make deletion decisions from stale state.

Solution: keep only null/token validation outside the lock, then check lf->running while holding lf->spinlock before finding and marking the entry for deletion.

Bug status: potential bug. The race requires concurrent log-forwarder shutdown and token deletion, but that is exactly the cleanup path this helper serves.

Functional impact: no intended behavior change. Deletion still returns true only when a live entry is found and marked.

Performance impact: negligible. The function already takes the spinlock for real work; the running-state read now shares that existing critical section.

(cherry picked from commit 35e56ad795b605556d0827862f77bdde7273293a)

* P1 perflib: reject instance names overlapping the instance header

The instance name bounds check kept the name inside the instance block but allowed NameOffset to point into the fixed PERF_INSTANCE_DEFINITION header, decoding header bytes as the name. Reject that only when a name exists, so unnamed instances (NameLength 0, NameOffset 0) remain valid.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* P1 spawn: check log forwarder running state under lock in annotate helpers

log_forwarder_annotate_token_name() and log_forwarder_annotate_token_pid() read lf->running before taking lf->spinlock, the same unsynchronized pattern already fixed for log_forwarder_del_and_close_token(). Keep only null/token validation outside the lock and check lf->running inside the critical section.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* spawn: document stderr fd ownership on token release

Clarify why spawn_server_release_stderr_fd() ignores the log_forwarder_del_and_close_token() return value: a valid token means the forwarder owns the fd and closes it on every path, so a fallback raw close could hit an unrelated recycled descriptor.

---------

Co-authored-by: Costa Tsaousis <costa@netdata.cloud>
Co-authored-by: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Stelios Fragkakis
message:
Netdata fixes part 55 (#23071)

* fix(xxhash): validate secret reset inputs before state mutation

XXH3_64bits_reset_withSecret() validated secret and secretSize after calling XXH3_reset_internal(). On invalid short secrets this was a potential error-path bug: the internal reset stored the external secret pointer and computed secretLimit = secretSize - XXH_STRIPE_LEN before the public API returned XXH_ERROR, so unsigned size_t underflow could leave corrupted state.

Move the existing null-secret and minimum-size checks before the internal reset, matching XXH3_64bits_reset_withSecretandSeed(). Valid calls keep the same reset path and return value. Invalid calls still return XXH_ERROR, but no longer partially mutate caller-owned state.

Functional impact: only invalid-input error paths change, by removing the partial state mutation. Performance impact: no hashing-loop impact; valid reset calls execute the same checks before the same reset work, and invalid calls do less work. Caller/contract impact: the only in-repo caller is XXH3_128bits_reset_withSecret(), which delegates and preserves the same return contract; external public-header callers keep the same OK/error contract.

(cherry picked from commit fc64073114ae7e70fcc25039a5176d092ec09f6c)

* fix(P0, eval): guard hardcoded variable rewrite progress

Current/potential bug: expression_hardcode_variable() can livelock when parsed variable matches remain nonzero but raw source replacement makes no progress, such as when a hardcoded variable name is a prefix of another token.

Functional impact: normal successful hardcoding remains unchanged; the pathological no-progress case now returns instead of consuming CPU indefinitely, leaving the source string unchanged rather than publishing a partial rewrite.

Performance impact: normal paths add one size_t snapshot and one equality comparison per rewrite loop iteration; the pathological path changes unbounded CPU use to bounded termination.

Caller impact: replace_green_red() regains control during health config parsing instead of stalling; eval hardcode tests and the API hardcode unittest keep their existing success paths.

Solution: detect a no-progress iteration after both raw replacement forms have been attempted and add a regression test for the prefix-token livelock trigger.
(cherry picked from commit 669dab0ee44e7924696cd54a8014e9066a0434e5)

* fix(P1, procfile): bound current readall growth

Issue: procfile_readall() loops while read() returns a positive byte count and grows the buffer whenever it fills. For finite proc/sys snapshot files this terminates, but a configurable procfile path or pseudo-file that never returns EOF can keep the loop alive indefinitely while memory keeps growing.

Current vs potential: current for procfile_readall() and current for configurable procfile paths that can point at never-EOF sources. The normal in-tree Linux proc/sys paths inspected by the worker are finite and below the new bound.

Solution: add a procfile-local 128 MiB source cap, clamp buffer growth and read size to the remaining cap, and route cap failures through the existing close-and-return-NULL error style with errno set to EFBIG.

Functional impact: finite procfile snapshot inputs below 128 MiB keep the existing read, parse, rewind, and NULL-on-error contract. Oversized or never-EOF sources now fail deterministically with EFBIG instead of livelocking and growing memory without bound.

Performance impact: normal paths add only size_t bound checks around each read iteration and a clamp when the buffer grows. Parser behavior, caller loops, locks, and public APIs are unchanged.

Caller impact: existing callers already handle procfile_readall() returning NULL on read/rewind failures. This commit adds one more defensive NULL-return path for pathological source size or non-terminating input.

Validation: git diff --check passed; minimal CMake configure passed; low-priority libnetdata build with -j2 passed. Full netdata target build was blocked after libnetdata by an unrelated missing ACLK generated schema input; ctest on the minimal tree reported no tests.

Reviewer gate: required reviewer process gate passed and all six required reviewer outputs ended with final approval verdicts. The first minimax output was excluded because it violated the read-only reviewer rule by creating scratch files under /tmp; a clean same-scope rerun approved.

(cherry picked from commit 1cdd83c2fe09c3e9ebb89fc09464025e9cc7b693)

* fix(P1, clocks): saturate heartbeat realtime deltas

Issue: MEM-clocks-003 flagged unsigned usec_t subtraction in heartbeat_next() for elapsed dt and heartbeat alignment stats.

Current/potential: current runtime bug. heartbeat_next() already handles now < next as a CLOCK_REALTIME-to-the-past case, but computed now - hb->realtime and now - next before/without ordering guards.

Solution: add an internal clocks_usec_delta_or_zero() helper, reuse it in the existing EINTR retry delta path, and apply it to the two vulnerable heartbeat_next() deltas.

Functional impact: normal and equal deltas are unchanged. Backward realtime or early post-sleep samples now produce 0 instead of UINT64-scale wrap.

Performance impact: one inline unsigned comparison per protected delta; no new syscalls, allocations, loops, locks, atomics, or scheduling changes.

Caller impact: direct heartbeat_next() callers keep the same API and sleep behavior. Returned elapsed values remain non-negative usec_t durations; callers already tolerate 0 on the first heartbeat and now avoid bogus wrapped elapsed time.

Validation: git diff --check passed; focused low-priority object compiles for clocks.c and clocks-unittest.c passed; standalone clocks_unittest runner passed; same-pattern scans found no remaining unguarded vulnerable heartbeat realtime delta.

Reviewer gate: six independent read-only reviews found the fix production-grade or mergeable with no blockers.

(cherry picked from commit 3a29553fc4bc3a23c8d19fe80252749f1856638d)

* fix(P1, dictionary): serialize pending delete accounting

Finding: MEM-dictionary-003

Current issue: dictionary deletion accounting can miss an item when the last reference release and the delete mark race. The old release path sampled ITEM_FLAG_DELETED before decrementing the final reference, while the delete-mark path only marked the item deleted. If the delete mark arrived after that sample, the item could reach zero references with ITEM_FLAG_DELETED set but without pending_deletion_items being incremented. Garbage collection is gated by that pending counter, so the deleted zero-ref item could remain linked and keep stale memory live.

Solution: add an explicit ITEM_FLAG_PENDING_DELETION bit and serialize delete-mark/final-release accounting with a small dictionary-local spinlock. The delete-mark path records pending deletion when it observes zero refs, and the final-release path records it when it observes a deleted item reaching zero refs. Pending state is cleared before freeing, so the counter and the item lifetime stay aligned. Stale-view rollback releases now use the same release helper without decrementing referenced_items.

Functional impact: dictionary API behavior, value ownership, hooks, index/list traversal, foreach, walkthrough, and view semantics are unchanged. The change only makes the internal pending-delete accounting reflect the existing deletion contract under a race that the current code can exercise.

Performance impact: lookup, ordinary acquire, and non-final release keep their existing fast paths. The new lock is taken only on delete marking, final-reference release, pending clear/free, and the rare deleted zero-to-one acquire path; single-threaded dictionaries skip it.

Validation: git diff --check passed for the touched files. A low-priority incremental build completed for libnetdata and netdata. A low-priority netdata -W dicttest run passed, including the new deterministic last-release/delete-mark accounting case and the existing dictionary concurrency tests.

Review gate: the first review round found a use-after-free risk in an earlier version of the patch. The implementation was corrected by clearing pending state before freeing, then the full scope was re-reviewed. Six independent read-only reviewer approvals were obtained; one incomplete reviewer output was rerun through an alternate read-only path and returned an explicit final approval.

(cherry picked from commit 752d1967adc7c76ddab43ac516f21f218012555d)

* fix(P1, uuidmap): preserve referenced destroy entries

Finding: DISC-uuidmap-destroy-referenced-free-20260707

Classification: current implementation bug in uuidmap_destroy(), with runtime exposure limited to callers that destroy UUIDMap while UUIDMAP_ID references still exist. The direct in-tree destroy caller is the ASAN shutdown cleanup path, but the implementation itself could free referenced entries and destroy backing state after reporting that references remained.

Root issue: uuidmap_destroy() counted entries for which refcount_acquire_for_deletion() failed, meaning they were still referenced, but still freed each entry and destroyed the Judy arrays, ARAL, partition state, and global uuid_map. Any outstanding UUIDMAP_ID holder could then use freed entry memory or release against destroyed backing state.

Solution: claim only zero-reference entries before freeing them, mirror the existing MRG teardown claim/restore pattern, and leave UUIDMap intact on a non-zero referenced return. If any referenced entry remains, claimed zero-reference entries are restored to refcount zero, the temporary claim array is freed, and all partition locks are released. If no referenced entries remain, claimed entries and all UUIDMap state are destroyed normally.

Functional impact: uuidmap_create(), uuidmap_dup(), uuidmap_free(), uuidmap_uuid(), uuidmap_uuid_ptr(), and uuidmap_uuid_ptr_and_dup() keep their existing behavior. The non-zero uuidmap_destroy() return contract is made safe: it now means UUIDMap state remains alive for outstanding holders instead of being destroyed underneath them.

Performance impact: no hot-path impact. Extra allocation and all-partition write locking happen only in uuidmap_destroy(), which is teardown-only and already walks every UUIDMap entry.

Caller impact: the shutdown caller already treats non-zero as a warning count and does not require UUIDMap state to be destroyed after that return. UUIDMap ownership flows in RRD dimensions, contexts, MRG/page descriptions, and transient lookup IDs keep the same create/dup/free contracts.

Validation: git diff --check passed for uuidmap.c and uuidmap.h. A low-priority incremental netdata build passed. A low-priority netdata -W uuidmaptest run passed, including the new referenced-destroy regression, the locked lookup/delete interleaving test, and the concurrent UUIDMap stress test.

Reviewer gate: the worker obtained five explicit read-only approvals, then failed closed because one reviewer output ended before a verdict. The missing read-only review was rerun through an alternate path with the same full scope and produced an explicit production-grade approval. No reviewer blockers remain.

(cherry picked from commit 4a69f65342525f05b2e0e95a7ddd70fca37de6b2)

* P1 uuidmap: keep destroy state under partition locks

Issue: uuidmap_destroy() released all partition locks before destroying the ARAL allocator and before clearing the global uuid_map storage. Any concurrent uuidmap user entering after the unlock could observe allocator teardown or partially cleared partition state.

Solution: keep all partition locks held until Judy arrays are freed, partition fields are reset, and the ARAL pointer is destroyed and nulled. Do not memset the whole global object after unlock-sensitive spinlock state has already been initialized.

Bug status: potential bug. The destroy path is rare, but the previous unlock-before-final-teardown ordering exposed invalid intermediate state to concurrent readers or writers.

Functional impact: no intended behavior change for normal uuid map operations. Destruction still returns the referenced-entry count and still refuses to destroy referenced entries.

Performance impact: none in steady state. The extra lock hold time happens only during uuidmap_destroy().

(cherry picked from commit 5dbab6f61c7c9b257b3803c06bb6eb4d1469c27a)

* P2 procfile: accept exact maximum sized inputs

Issue: procfile_readall() rejected files whose size was exactly PROCFILE_MAX_SOURCE_SIZE because it used s >= limit before checking whether the file had ended. The documented hard limit should reject bytes beyond the limit, not the exact boundary itself.

Solution: reject only s > limit immediately. At s == limit, perform a one-byte EOF probe: EOF accepts the exact-sized file, any extra byte takes the existing file-too-large path, and read errors keep the existing error handling.

Bug status: current bug for any procfile source that is exactly 128 MiB.

Functional impact: narrow intended behavior change. Exact-limit files become valid; oversized files remain rejected.

Performance impact: none on normal reads. The extra read happens only when the file has already reached the hard maximum size.

(cherry picked from commit 9d4b458a7c3ec1ea9defa2ed6830347b28477f2b)

* P2 procfile: keep a spare byte when accepting exact maximum sized inputs

The exact-limit EOF probe broke out of the read loop with ff->len == ff->size, so procfile_parser() had no room for the terminating '\0' of the last word and clobbered the file's last data byte. Reserve one extra buffer byte on that path so exact maximum sized inputs are parsed intact.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* P1 uuidmap: make the referenced-destroy unittest baseline-relative

In the full 'netdata -W unittest' sequence, earlier tests still hold referenced uuidmap entries, so asserting absolute counts (1, then 0) failed in CI with 1134 pre-existing entries. Measure the baseline first and assert relative to it; standalone '-W uuidmaptest' behavior is unchanged (baseline 0).

* P1 uuidmap: initialize the ARAL under the partition write lock

uuidmap_create() initialized the ARAL before taking the partition lock, so a concurrent uuidmap_destroy() (which holds all partition locks) could destroy and NULL it before the creator reached aral_mallocz(). Initialize it at the point of use instead: holding any partition write lock excludes destroy, so the allocator cannot disappear while it is used.

---------

Co-authored-by: Costa Tsaousis <costa@netdata.cloud>
Co-authored-by: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: nedi-app[bot]
message:
docs: Clarify Cloud alert edits apply instantly and override stock files (#23008)

* docs: Added a user-facing note to dynamic-configuration.md clarifying that UI alert/collector edits take effect immediately on submit (no scheduled synchronization) and are stored as dynamic overrides that do not modify the stock health.d/*.conf files. Directly resolves the user's questions about whether Cloud edits propagate to C:\Program Files\Netdata\usr\lib\netdata\conf.d\health.d\ and how long synchronization takes.

* Apply suggestion from @Ancairon

author: nedi-app[bot]
message:
docs: Add Windows config and health.d alert template path guidance (#23003)

* docs: Added a Windows subsection to the 'Locate Your Config Directory' section of the Agent configuration README, documenting the Windows Netdata config directory, where the default (stock) alert templates ship, where user alert overrides go, that edit-config is Linux-only (edit files directly on Windows), the Windows-native reload command, and a cross-reference to the health configuration reference for alert syntax.

* docs: Fix Windows edit-config guidance in Agent configuration README

Correct the Windows subsection's inaccurate edit-config claim and
cross-reference gaps introduced in PR #23003: edit-config works on
Windows via the bundled MSYS2 shell (see WINDOWS_INSTALLER.md), not
"Linux-only"; note that Program Files requires Administrator
privileges to save changes there; and cross-link the Windows path from
the Edit Configuration Files section so it isn't Linux-only in effect.

* docs: Move Windows alert-editing content to the health configuration reference

Alert-specific details (stock/override health.d paths, reload-health)
belonged in src/health/REFERENCE.md, the canonical alerts doc, not in
the general Agent configuration README, which stays generic on the
Linux side. Trim the Windows note there to just the config-directory
info, and add the Windows-specific alert paths and reload command to
REFERENCE.md's existing How-To Guides, next to their Linux equivalents.

Also dedupes a pre-existing repeated "Configuration Locations:" line
in the section touched.

---------

Co-authored-by: nedi-app[bot] <nedi-app[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>
Co-authored-by: Ancairon <fotis@netdata.cloud>

author: nedi-app[bot]
message:
docs: Add hostname display guidance to Group by Node in charts (#23012)

docs: Clarified the 'Group by Node' option in the Group By Dropdown section so users discover it displays each node's hostname as a labeled dimension in the chart legend (Dimensions Bar). Revised the table row description to bridge the 'hostname' (user intent) vs 'Group by Node' (feature name) terminology gap, and added a tip admonition that directly answers how to see hostnames on the chart.

Co-authored-by: nedi-app[bot] <nedi-app[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>

author: Costa Tsaousis
message: fix group-by percentage at the second grouping level with a dimensions filter (#23104)

author: nedi-app[bot]
message:
docs: Add per-app network bandwidth pointer to apps.plugin README (#23001)

* docs: Added a user-facing pointer in apps.plugin README 'Integration with eBPF' section describing how to monitor per-application network bandwidth (upload/download) via the eBPF Socket collector when the apps integration option is enabled in ebpf.d/network.conf.

* docs: Clarify eBPF Socket prerequisite for per-app bandwidth chart

The socket eBPF program is disabled by default, so enabling only the
apps option in network.conf produces no chart. Point readers to the
[ebpf programs] section first.

---------

Co-authored-by: nedi-app[bot] <nedi-app[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>
